verifier_values.criteria: States that the symptoms are consistent with heart failure.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
“Patient: I’ve been struggling to breathe, especially over the last few weeks. Even sitting still, I sometimes feel like I can’t get enough air.
Doctor: Is it worse when you’re lying flat?
Patient: Yes. I’ve had to sleep propped up with three pillows. If I lie flat, I feel like I’m suffocating.
Doctor: Have you woken up at night short of breath?
Patient: A few times. I wake up gasping and have to sit on the edge of the bed.
Doctor: That sounds very uncomfortable. Have you noticed any swelling in your legs or ankles?
Patient: Yes, my ankles are quite swollen by the evening, and my shoes feel tight.
Doctor: Any weight gain recently?
Patient: About four kilos in the last month, even though I’m not eating more.
Doctor: Have you had chest pain or tightness?
Patient: Sometimes when I walk even a short distance. It eases when I stop.
Doctor: Any dizziness or blackouts?
Patient: I get lightheaded, especially when I stand up quickly, but I haven’t passed out.“
“Doctor: I can see swelling in your legs and hear crackles at the bases of your lungs, which suggests fluid build-up. When I listen to your heart, I hear a loud, harsh murmur when the heart is pumping blood out, and your pulse rises more slowly than expected.
Patient: What does that mean?
Doctor: Taken together, your symptoms and exam findings suggest that your heart is struggling to pump blood forward effectively, and fluid is backing up into your lungs and legs. This is what we call congestive cardiac failure.
Patient: Why would that happen?
Doctor: There are several possible causes. One of my main concerns, given the murmur and your symptoms, is that the aortic valve may be severely narrowed. When that valve doesn’t open properly, the heart has to work much harder, and eventually it can start to fail.
Patient: Is that dangerous?
Doctor: It can be serious, especially when symptoms like breathlessness, swelling, and chest pain are present. However, we need to confirm the exact cause before deciding on treatment.”
Extract from script. 

“A 78-year-old gentleman presents to the emergency department with shortness of breath”
Extract from the prompt.

“Main symptoms
The most common symptoms of heart failure are:
breathlessness – this may occur after activity or at rest; it may be worse when you're lying down, and you may wake up at night needing to catch your breath
fatigue – you may feel tired most of the time and find exercise exhausting
swollen ankles and legs – this is caused by a build-up of fluid (oedema); it may be better in the morning and get worse later in the day
feeling lightheaded and fainting”
https://www.nhs.uk/conditions/heart-failure/symptoms/

verifier_values.criteria: States that the symptoms are consistent with aortic stenosis.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
“Patient: I’ve been struggling to breathe, especially over the last few weeks. Even sitting still, I sometimes feel like I can’t get enough air.
Doctor: Is it worse when you’re lying flat?
Patient: Yes. I’ve had to sleep propped up with three pillows. If I lie flat, I feel like I’m suffocating.
Doctor: Have you woken up at night short of breath?
Patient: A few times. I wake up gasping and have to sit on the edge of the bed.
Doctor: That sounds very uncomfortable. Have you noticed any swelling in your legs or ankles?
Patient: Yes, my ankles are quite swollen by the evening, and my shoes feel tight.
Doctor: Any weight gain recently?
Patient: About four kilos in the last month, even though I’m not eating more.
Doctor: Have you had chest pain or tightness?
Patient: Sometimes when I walk even a short distance. It eases when I stop.
Doctor: Any dizziness or blackouts?
Patient: I get lightheaded, especially when I stand up quickly, but I haven’t passed out.“
“Doctor: I can see swelling in your legs and hear crackles at the bases of your lungs, which suggests fluid build-up. When I listen to your heart, I hear a loud, harsh murmur when the heart is pumping blood out, and your pulse rises more slowly than expected.
Patient: What does that mean?
Doctor: Taken together, your symptoms and exam findings suggest that your heart is struggling to pump blood forward effectively, and fluid is backing up into your lungs and legs. This is what we call congestive cardiac failure.
Patient: Why would that happen?
Doctor: There are several possible causes. One of my main concerns, given the murmur and your symptoms, is that the aortic valve may be severely narrowed. When that valve doesn’t open properly, the heart has to work much harder, and eventually it can start to fail.
Patient: Is that dangerous?
Doctor: It can be serious, especially when symptoms like breathlessness, swelling, and chest pain are present. However, we need to confirm the exact cause before deciding on treatment.”
Extract from script. 

“A 78-year-old gentleman presents to the emergency department with shortness of breath”
Extract from prompt.

“Aortic stenosis causes impaired outflow of blood from the heart and is usually progressive. The increased cardiac workload leads to left ventricular hypertrophy and heart failure. Symptoms of aortic stenosis typically include shortness of breath and chest pain on exertion. Mortality rates are high in symptomatic patients.”
https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ipg586/chapter/2-Indications-and-current-treatments

verifier_values.criteria: States that the ECHO confirms severe aortic stenosis.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
“Dilated LV cavity size with impaired systolic function. LVEF estimated at 32% visually. There is LVH.”
“severe aortic stenosis: AV peak velocity 4.2 m/s, peak gradient 44 mmHg, AVA 0.8 cm2”
Extract from echo

“1.2.8
Perform transthoracic echocardiography to exclude important valve disease, assess the systolic (and diastolic) function of the left ventricle and detect intracardiac shunts.”
https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng106/chapter/Recommendations#diagnosing-heart-failure

“Aortic stenosis causes impaired outflow of blood from the heart and is usually progressive. The increased cardiac workload leads to left ventricular hypertrophy and heart failure. Symptoms of aortic stenosis typically include shortness of breath and chest pain on exertion. Mortality rates are high in symptomatic patients.”
https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ipg586/chapter/2-Indications-and-current-treatments

verifier_values.criteria: States that the patient developed hyperkalemia while being on an ACE inhibitor.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
“The gentleman was started on an ACE inhibitor, but it was stopped due to high potassium.”
Extract from prompt.

verifier_values.criteria: States that second-line therapy should be considered.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
Second line therapy includes - Digoxin, Angiotensin receptor neprolysin inhibitors, Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist , Hydralazine and nitrate , Ivabradine.
“Overview
This guideline covers diagnosing and managing chronic heart failure in people aged 18 and over. It aims to improve diagnosis and treatment to increase the length and quality of life for people with heart failure.”
https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng106

“If symptoms persist, seek specialist advice and consider:”
https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng106/resources/chronic-heart-failure-core-treatments-for-heart-failure-pdf-6663137725

verifier_values.criteria: States that digoxin is ruled out due to documented allergy.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
“and has a digoxin allergy.”
Extract from prompt.

“Offer digoxin to people with worsening or severe heart failure with reduced ejection fraction despite optimised treatment combinations as detailed in recommendations 1.4.1 to 1.4.4. Seek specialist advice before starting treatment. [2010, amended 2025]“
https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng106/chapter/Recommendations#treating-people-with-newly-diagnosed-and-pre-existing-heart-failure-with-reduced-ejection-fraction

verifier_values.criteria: States that mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA) is contraindicated in the setting of hyperkalemia.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA) is ruled out because of hyperkalemia.

MRAs:
“Spironolactone
Contra-indications
Contra-indicationsFor spironolactone
Addison’s disease; anuria; hyperkalaemia
Monitoring of patient parametersFor spironolactone
Monitor electrolytes—discontinue if hyperkalaemia occurs (in severe heart failure monitor potassium ”
https://bnf.nice.org.uk/drugs/spironolactone/

verifier_values.criteria: States that hydralazine-nitrate dual therapy is contraindicated in severe aortic stenosis.
verifier_values.negative_criteria: States that hydralazine-nitrate dual therapy  can be used in severe aortic stenosis.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
“nitrates are not recommended in patients with severe AS”
https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/circulationaha.116.023997
and
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24589852/

HYDRALAZINE AND NITRATES in patients with LVSD:
“BOX 1: CONTRAINDICATIONS AND CAUTIONS CONTRAINDICATIONS • Hypersensitivity to nitrates and or hydralazine (and any excipients) • Acute circulatory failure (shock, vascular collapse) • Severe tachycardia and heart failure with a high cardiac output (e.g. in thyrotoxicosis). • Conditions with fixed cardiac output: Hypertrophic (obstructive) cardiomyopathy, severe aortic stenosis, cardiac tamponade, constrictive pericarditis, mitral stenosis (unless under specialist supervision),”
https://swlimo.southwestlondon.icb.nhs.uk/wp-content/uploads/2022/02/HEART-FAILURE-Pharmacological-Management-of-Heart-failure.pdf

verifier_values.criteria: States that angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs) are contraindicated in the setting of hyperkalemia.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
ARBs
“For all angiotensin II receptor antagonists
Common or very common
Abdominal pain; asthenia; back pain; cough; diarrhoea; dizziness; headache; hyperkalaemia; hypotension; nausea; postural hypotension (more common in patients with intravascular volume depletion, e.g. those taking high-dose diuretics); renal impairment; vertigo; vomiting”
https://bnf.nice.org.uk/drugs/candesartan-cilexetil/

ARBs:
“angiotensin II receptor antagonists
Elderly
Screening Tool of Older Persons' potentially inappropriate Prescriptions (STOPP) criteria to aid medication reviews (see Prescribing in the elderly for information). Potentially inappropriate:
•	with hyperkalaemia”
https://bnf.nice.org.uk/drugs/candesartan-cilexetil/

verifier_values.criteria: States that angiotensin receptor neprolysin inhibitors (ARNIs) are contraindicated in the setting of hyperkalemia.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
“Contra-indications
For eplerenone
Hyperkalaemia”
https://bnf.nice.org.uk/drugs/eplerenone/#contra-indications

ANRIs
“Side-effects
Side-effects For eplerenone
Angioedema; arterial thrombosis; cholecystitis; eosinophilia; flatulence; gynaecomastia; hyperhidrosis; hypothyroidism; increased risk of infection; malaise; numbness; postural hypotension”

verifier_values.criteria: States that Ivabradine is the most appropriate medication for this patient at this time.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
Ivabradine is the best choice as the patient have sinus rhythm, along with ejection fraction of 35% or less. ‘Specialist treatment
Ivabradine
1.4.8
Ivabradine is recommended as an option in NICE technology appraisal guidance for treating heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. For full details, see the guidance on ivabradine (TA267, 2012).”
https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng106/chapter/Recommendations#treating-people-with-newly-diagnosed-and-pre-existing-heart-failure-with-reduced-ejection-fraction

“Ivabradine, if sinus rhythym and heart rate of 75 beats per minute or more and ejection fraction is 35% or less”
https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng106/resources/chronic-heart-failure-core-treatments-for-heart-failure-pdf-6663137725

verifier_values.criteria: States that the starting dose would be 2.5mg twice daily.
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
“Mild to severe chronic heart failure for ivabradine
By mouth
Adult 18–74 years
Initially 5 mg twice daily for 2 weeks, then increased if necessary to 7.5 mg twice daily; reduced if not tolerated to 2.5–5 mg twice daily, heart rate at rest should not be allowed to fall below 50 beats per minute.
Adult 75 years and over
Initially 2.5 mg twice daily for 2 weeks, then increased if necessary up to 7.5 mg twice daily, dose to be increased gradually; reduced if not tolerated to 2.5–5 mg twice daily, heart rate at rest should not be allowed to fall below 50 beats per minute.”
https://bnf.nice.org.uk/drugs/ivabradine/
